The documentation of `tolist` says:

> Return a copy of the array data as a (nested) Python list. Data items are converted to the nearest compatible builtin Python type, via the [item](https://numpy.org/doc/1.26/reference/generated/numpy.ndarray.item.html#numpy.ndarray.item) function.

So, I expect it to return something along the lines of `[v.item() for v in x]` (somewhat simplified, of course). 

Another quote from the docs:
> For a 1D array, a.tolist() is almost the same as list(a), except that tolist changes numpy scalars to Python scalars:

This behaviour could cause non-converging iterations for definitely well-posed problems.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20168/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20168/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/1972969672","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20168#issuecomment-1972969672","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20168","id":1972969672,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc51mSDI","user":{"login":"ezander","id":111723,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTcyMw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/111723?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander","html_url":"https://github.com/ezander","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ezander/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-03-01T10:54:28Z","updated_at":"2024-03-01T10:54:28Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> Hi @ezander do you have any examples where this actually causes any errors? 
Yes, I do. I used nnls for fitting some piecewise linear function to some data using monotonicity constraints. And in very few of the cases the algorithm did not converge (or rather threw some RuntimeError), which I could trace back finally to the inner loop of `_nnls`. I could put that into a unit test, however, it is a bit messy and I was unable to extract some nice small MWE from it. 

> Because hitting actual zeros are never feasible in floating point arithmetic hence we have to assume things zero if they are small enough. That's why a tol is used. If the inequality was strict indeed we would have used zero instead of tol.
Sure, but we're not testing for equality to zero here. If it was `s[P].min()==0` and you would turn that into `-tol<s[P].min()<tol`, I'd be totally fine, but here the check is `s[P].min()<0`, which checks for an interval $(-\infty,0)$ anyway, so there is no need for additional tolerance IMHO. 

And, BTW, I think the inequality should be strict, because it's purpose is to check for a constraint violation, which is only given if some `s` is negative, not when it's just zero.

This behaviour could cause non-converging iterations for definitely well-posed problems. 
I have `s[P].min() <= 0`.

It now quits the inner loop after having removed some passive variables, but then enters it again after having set them passive again. E.g. in the new unit test I pushed, when I trace the passive variables I get this (just an extract, so this doesn't get too long)
```
<snip>
```

The indices 0 to 5 are added one by one to the passive set in the outer loop, but somehow as soon as number 5 is added, they become (slightly) negative and all are removed in the inner loop. Then the whole cycle starts anew in the outer loop.

I haven't found a clean way to solve this yet, except currently just setting the tolerance higher.

It still went into some infinite loop.
Would be okay to put those variables, that were set passive and then active again, into some kind of "permanent active" set, such that they just stay there and won't get added (and removed) again? But I don't know whether there are cases where this happens, say two times, and then stabilizes and leads to the termination of the algorithm and the correct solution.
Maybe your solution with `-tol` would work but with another value of `tol`? I'm not sure, but it could be related to the condition number of the sub-matrix of A given by the passive set, but it would take some time to come up with a good theory here, to support that. 
Furthermore, we could run into trouble because at termination some variables could be slightly negative and some algorithms which need `nnls` are fine with that, but others aren't and need complete non-negativity, maybe sacrificing some optimality for it.
Just my two cents...
